_meta.description: EPSO AD/426/25 · Data Management · Seed v1 (20 MCQs).

mcqs.question: When did the General Data Protection Regulation (Reg. (EU) 2016/679) become applicable?

options.A: 27 April 2016
options.D: 24 September 2023

explanations.A: 27 April 2016 is the adoption date, not the application date — GDPR provided a two-year transition period.
explanations.B: Correct. GDPR became applicable on 25 May 2018, two years after adoption (Art. 99).
explanations.C: 11 December 2018 is the application date of Regulation 2018/1725 (EU institutions), not GDPR.
explanations.D: 24 September 2023 is the application date of the Data Governance Act (Reg. 2022/868).

mcqs.question: Which regulation governs the processing of personal data by EU institutions, bodies, offices and agencies?

options.A: Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR)
options.B: Regulation (EU) 2018/1725
options.D: Regulation (EU) 2022/868 (DGA)

explanations.A: GDPR explicitly excludes EU institutions from its material scope (Art. 2).
explanations.B: Correct. Reg. 2018/1725 mirrors the GDPR rules for EU institutions; the EDPS supervises compliance.
explanations.C: The Open Data Directive governs reuse of public-sector information, not personal-data protection.
explanations.D: The DGA sets rules for data intermediation and altruism, not the personal-data protection regime of EU institutions.

mcqs.question: Under the Open Data Directive (2019/1024), High-Value Datasets are defined in an Implementing Regulation with six thematic categories. Which of the following is NOT one of them?

options.A: Geospatial
options.B: Meteorological
options.D: Mobility

explanations.A: Geospatial is one of the six HVD categories (Implementing Reg. 2023/138).
explanations.B: Meteorological is one of the six categories.
explanations.C: Correct. Health is NOT an HVD category — electronic health data is governed by the EHDS (Reg. 2025/327). The six HVD categories are: geospatial; earth observation & environment; meteorological; statistics; companies & company ownership; mobility.
explanations.D: Mobility is one of the six categories.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four pillars of the Data Governance Act (Reg. (EU) 2022/868)?

options.A: Reuse of protected public-sector data
options.B: Data intermediation services
options.D: Cloud-to-cloud switching rights

explanations.A: Pillar 1 — rules for reusing protected public-sector data (confidential, IP-covered, personal).
explanations.B: Pillar 2 — providers of data intermediation services under a notification regime (Ch III).
explanations.C: Pillar 3 — data altruism for causes of general interest, with a standardised European Data Altruism Consent Form (Ch IV).
explanations.D: Correct (the trap). Cloud/edge switching rights belong to the Data Act (Ch VI), not the DGA. The DGA's fourth pillar is the European Data Innovation Board (EDIB).

mcqs.question: A manufacturer of connected coffee machines must comply with which chapter of the Data Act (Reg. (EU) 2023/2854) when the user asks to access the usage data the machine generates?

options.A: Chapter II — access to product data by users
options.B: Chapter III — B2B data sharing on FRAND terms
options.C: Chapter V — B2G in cases of exceptional need
options.D: Chapter VI — cloud and edge switching

explanations.A: Correct. Ch II of the Data Act gives the user of a connected product the right to access the data the product generates.
explanations.B: Ch III governs data sharing between businesses on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms — not user access.
explanations.C: Ch V covers public-body access in emergencies or situations of exceptional public interest — not a consumer request.
explanations.D: Ch VI is about switching between cloud / edge providers; unrelated to IoT user-data access.

mcqs.question: Under the AI Act (Reg. (EU) 2024/1689), a real-time remote biometric identification system used in public spaces for law-enforcement purposes is categorised as:

options.A: Minimal risk
options.B: Limited risk
options.D: Unacceptable risk (banned, with narrow exceptions)

explanations.A: Minimal risk covers the bulk of AI (spam filters, games) with voluntary obligations only.
explanations.B: Limited risk triggers transparency duties (e.g. chatbots disclosing they are AI).
explanations.C: High-risk AI (Annex III) requires conformity assessment, but this specific use is further up the pyramid.
explanations.D: Correct. Art 5 lists real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces for law-enforcement as an unacceptable practice, banned save narrowly defined exceptions (serious crime, prior authorisation).

mcqs.question: Article 10 of the AI Act imposes data-governance requirements on training, validation and test datasets of high-risk AI systems. Which obligation is specifically listed by Article 10?

options.A: Mandatory open-data publication of all training datasets
options.B: Examination of possible biases and appropriate measures to detect, prevent and mitigate them
options.D: Blanket prohibition of any personal data in training sets

explanations.A: There is no blanket obligation to publish training datasets openly; trade secrets and IP are protected.
explanations.B: Correct. Art 10(2)(f) requires examination of possible biases likely to affect health, safety or fundamental rights, with appropriate detection, prevention and mitigation measures.
explanations.C: Synthetic data is permitted but not required; the key criterion is relevance and representativeness.
explanations.D: Personal data may be processed in training sets under specific safeguards (Art 10(5)); there is no outright ban.

mcqs.question: The 'Data Union Strategy' announced in November 2025 is:

options.A: A binding regulation on Common European Data Spaces
options.B: A directive transposing the Data Act into national law
options.D: A joint statement of the European Data Protection Board

explanations.A: It is not a regulation — it has no binding legal force on its own.
explanations.B: The Data Act is a regulation and does not require transposition; no directive is derived from it.
explanations.C: Correct. The Data Union Strategy is a Commission Communication (COM(2025) 835), a policy framework succeeding the 2020 European Strategy for Data.
explanations.D: The EDPB issues guidelines on GDPR, not EU data-market strategies.

mcqs.question: In the DAMA-DMBOK framework, which knowledge area is depicted at the centre of the wheel, connecting all others?

options.A: Data Architecture
options.B: Data Quality
options.D: Metadata

explanations.A: Data Architecture is one of the 11 knowledge areas but sits on the periphery, not the hub.
explanations.B: Data Quality is a peripheral knowledge area.
explanations.C: Correct. Data Governance is the central, cross-cutting knowledge area binding the other ten.
explanations.D: Metadata management is an important but peripheral knowledge area.

mcqs.question: The FAIR principles for data stewardship stand for:

options.A: Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable
options.B: Fast, Aggregated, Integrated, Relational
options.D: Formal, Authoritative, Immutable, Reproducible

explanations.A: Correct. FAIR = Findable · Accessible · Interoperable · Reusable (Wilkinson et al., 2016).
explanations.B: Not the FAIR definition; these are unrelated data-engineering adjectives.
explanations.C: Plausible but incorrect — 'Federated' and 'Audited' are not part of FAIR.
explanations.D: Reproducibility is related but not the FAIR wording; the fourth letter is Reusable.

mcqs.question: The European Interoperability Framework (EIF) defines four interoperability layers. Which is the correct order, from 'most policy' to 'most technical'?

options.A: Technical · Semantic · Organisational · Legal
options.B: Legal · Organisational · Semantic · Technical
options.D: Semantic · Technical · Legal · Organisational

explanations.A: Reversed. EIF layers are ordered legal → technical.
explanations.B: Correct. Legal · Organisational · Semantic · Technical, with an Interoperability Governance overlay.
explanations.C: Incorrect ordering — Legal precedes Organisational.
explanations.D: Does not reflect the EIF structure.

mcqs.question: The CIA triad in information security refers to:

options.A: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
options.B: Compliance, Integrity, Auditability
options.D: Consent, Isolation, Accountability

explanations.A: Correct. Confidentiality · Integrity · Availability are the three pillars of the CIA model; sometimes extended with Authenticity / Non-repudiation.
explanations.B: Auditability is a control, not a core CIA objective.
explanations.C: These relate to access management (e.g. RBAC), not CIA.
explanations.D: Consent is a GDPR lawful basis, not part of the CIA triad.

mcqs.question: Under the GDPR, a payroll service running employee salaries on behalf of a company is typically:

options.A: The data controller, because it physically handles the data
options.B: The data processor, because it processes data on behalf of the controller
options.C: Jointly controller and processor by default
options.D: Outside GDPR scope since pay slips are considered public

explanations.A: Controllership is defined by who determines purposes and means, not by who physically handles data.
explanations.B: Correct. The company determines purposes and means (controller); the payroll service acts on its instructions (processor, Art 4(8), Art 28).
explanations.C: Joint controllership (Art 26) requires joint determination of purposes and means — not the default for a service provider.
explanations.D: Pay slips contain personal data of employees and are in GDPR scope regardless of who sees them.

mcqs.question: What is DCAT-AP primarily used for on the EU data.europa.eu portal?

options.A: A machine-learning model registry for EU projects
options.B: An application profile of the DCAT vocabulary to describe datasets and catalogues
options.C: A file format for personal-data export requests under GDPR
options.D: A public directory of SaaS cloud providers

explanations.A: DCAT-AP is not a model registry; no such EU standard uses this name.
explanations.B: Correct. DCAT-AP (Data Catalogue Vocabulary Application Profile) specifies how to describe datasets on European data portals; extensions include StatDCAT-AP and GeoDCAT-AP.
explanations.C: GDPR data portability (Art 20) uses common structured formats but is not specified by DCAT-AP.
explanations.D: DCAT-AP describes datasets and catalogues, not cloud SaaS providers.

mcqs.question: A data team adopts ELT instead of ETL when ingesting logs into a modern cloud warehouse. The key difference is that in ELT:

options.A: Transformations happen before loading into the target store
options.B: Transformations happen inside the target store after loading
options.C: No transformations are applied at all
options.D: Data is loaded but never queried

explanations.A: That describes the classic ETL pattern, not ELT.
explanations.B: Correct. In ELT the raw data is loaded into the target (e.g. a columnar warehouse) and transformed there, leveraging the warehouse's compute.
explanations.C: ELT still transforms — just after loading.
explanations.D: Loading without querying is archival, not ELT.

mcqs.question: Which statement about the GDPR treatment of anonymised vs pseudonymised data is correct?

options.A: Both fall outside GDPR scope once processed
options.B: Pseudonymised data falls outside GDPR; anonymised data stays in scope
options.C: Anonymised data falls outside GDPR; pseudonymised data remains in scope
options.D: Both are always in GDPR scope regardless of technique

explanations.A: Only true anonymisation removes data from scope; pseudonymisation does not.
explanations.B: Reversed — it is anonymisation that takes data out of scope.
explanations.C: Correct. Anonymisation (irreversible) means the data is no longer personal and is outside GDPR (Recital 26). Pseudonymisation (reversible with a key) is a safeguard, but the data remains personal and in scope (Art 4(5), Art 25, Art 32).
explanations.D: Properly executed anonymisation is explicitly recognised as taking data out of scope (Recital 26).

mcqs.question: An analytics team needs ACID transactions, schema enforcement and time-travel directly on object storage holding raw Parquet files, while still supporting BI dashboards and ML feature pipelines. Which architecture best fits?

options.A: A classic data warehouse with nightly batch ingestion
options.B: A Hadoop-era data lake with schema-on-read only
options.C: A lakehouse using an open table format such as Delta, Iceberg or Hudi
options.D: A relational OLTP database with read replicas

explanations.A: A classic warehouse uses proprietary storage and does not operate directly on open object-storage Parquet.
explanations.B: A plain lake lacks ACID, schema enforcement and time-travel; schema-on-read alone is limiting for BI.
explanations.C: Correct. A lakehouse layers an open table format (Delta / Iceberg / Hudi) on object-store files, providing ACID, schema enforcement and time-travel while keeping the openness of a lake, serving both BI and ML.
explanations.D: OLTP with replicas is designed for transactional workloads, not analytics on large Parquet files.

mcqs.question: Which statement best distinguishes a KPI from an ordinary metric?

options.A: A KPI must always be expressed as a percentage
options.B: A KPI is a metric chosen for its strategic link to an objective and used to track progress
options.C: A KPI is only valid if refreshed in real time
options.D: A KPI is a synonym for a dashboard

explanations.A: KPIs can be counts, ratios, currency — not only percentages.
explanations.B: Correct. A Key Performance Indicator is a metric deliberately chosen because it ties to an organisational objective and tracks progress toward it.
explanations.C: Refresh cadence depends on the use case — many KPIs are tracked monthly or quarterly.
explanations.D: A dashboard is a visualisation that may display KPIs; the two terms are not synonymous.

mcqs.question: In a supervised machine-learning pipeline for fraud detection, 'feature engineering' refers to which activity?

options.A: Assigning the 'fraud / not fraud' ground-truth to each historical record
options.B: Creating predictive variables (e.g. rolling-average amount, device-country mismatch flag) from raw data
options.C: Evaluating model performance on a held-out test set
options.D: Defining role-based access controls for the model's training data

explanations.A: That describes data labelling — assigning the target class to examples.
explanations.B: Correct. Feature engineering designs and derives the input variables the model will learn from, drawing on domain knowledge.
explanations.C: Evaluating on a test set is model validation, not feature engineering.
explanations.D: RBAC governs access to data but has nothing to do with feature creation.

mcqs.question: Which of the following correctly pairs EU multilingual reference resources with their primary purpose?

options.A: IATE — a controlled thesaurus used to index EU publications; EuroVoc — the EU terminology database
options.B: EuroVoc — the EU's inter-institutional terminology database; IATE — a multilingual thesaurus
options.C: IATE — the EU's inter-institutional terminology database; EuroVoc — a multilingual thesaurus for indexing EU documents
options.D: IATE — a public-procurement catalogue; EuroVoc — the EU's translation memory

explanations.A: Swaps the roles — IATE is a terminology database, not a thesaurus.
explanations.B: Swaps the roles — EuroVoc is a thesaurus, not a terminology database.
explanations.C: Correct. IATE (Inter-Active Terminology for Europe) is the EU's inter-institutional terminology database; EuroVoc is the multilingual thesaurus used to index EU documents across institutions.
explanations.D: Neither is a procurement catalogue; the EU translation memory is Euramis, not EuroVoc.
